perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to the jurisdiction and venue of courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'exclusive' and specifying Florida and Georgia courts could allow the defendant to pursue legal action in other locations, which may violate legal precedents regarding the consumer's right to choose a convenient forum, especially if the other party has significantly more resources. This could impose undue hardship on the consumer.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. By removing the term 'exclusive' and the specification of Florida and Georgia courts, the modified text might be interpreted as an attempt to prevent a consumer from requesting a more convenient venue, potentially contradicting the spirit and application of this law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts'. The changed text removes 'exclusive' and the specific states, potentially allowing actions to be brought in any court. 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), as shown in both snippets, allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. The original contract limits this right by specifying exclusive venues. Removing this exclusivity and the specific locations means a consumer could be forced to litigate in a distant, inconvenient venue, undermining the consumer protection implied by § 1404(a), thus creating a contradiction. The original agreement provides a level of certainty and protection that is lost in the altered version.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any dispute under or arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party in such dispute shall be entitled to recover from the non-prevailing party or parties, in addition to any damages and/or other relief that may be awarded, its reasonable costs and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) incurred in connection with prosecuting or defending the subject dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any dispute under or arising out of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own costs and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees. While seemingly neutral, this could violate consumer protection laws that mandate fee-shifting in certain cases to protect consumers from bearing excessive legal costs when vindicating their rights, deterring them from pursuing valid claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act allows a consumer who successfully sues for breach of warranty to recover reasonable attorney's fees and costs. The modified clause, by requiring each party to bear its own costs, directly contradicts this provision, potentially preventing a consumer from recovering fees they are legally entitled to.

Remedies in consumer disputes (a) Informal dispute settlement procedures; establishment; rules setting forth minimum requirements; effect of compliance by warrantor; review of informal procedures or implementation by Commission; application to existing informal procedures (1) Congress hereby declares it to be its policy to encourage warrantors to establish procedures whereby consumer disputes are fairly and expeditiously settled through informal dispute settlement
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 2310 - Remedies in consumer disputes | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2310 - Remedies in consumer disputes U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Informal dispute settlement procedures; establishment; rules setting forth minimum requirements; effect of compliance by warrantor; review of informal procedures or implementation by Commission; application to existing informal procedures (1) Congress hereby declares it to be its policy to encourage warrantors to establish procedures whereby consumer disputes are fairly and expeditiously settled through informal dispute settlement mechanisms. (2) The Commission shall prescribe rules setting forth minimum requirements for any informal dispute settlement procedure which is incorporated into the terms of a written warranty to which any provision of this chapter applies. Such rules shall provide for participation in such procedure by independent or governmental entities. (3) One or more warrantors may establish an informal dispute settlement procedure which meets the requirements of the Commission ’s rules under paragraph (2). If— (A) a warrantor establishes such a procedure, (B) such procedure, and its implementation, meets the requirements of such rules, and (C) he incorporates in a written warranty a requirement that the consumer resort to such procedure before pursuing any legal remedy under this section respecting such warranty, then (i) the consumer may not commence a civil action (other than a class action) under subsection (d) of this section unless he initially resorts to such procedure; and (ii) a class of consumers may not proceed in a class action under subsection (d) except to the extent the court determines necessary to establish the representative capacity of the named plaintiffs, unless the named plaintiffs (upon notifying the defendant that they are named plaintiffs in a cla
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the law by removing the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees, which may violate consumer protection laws, specifically the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, as explained by the LLM. This act allows consumers who successfully sue for breach of warranty to recover reasonable attorney's fees and costs. The modified clause, by stating each party shall bear its own costs, directly contradicts this provision, potentially preventing a consumer from recovering fees they are legally entitled to. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions remedies in consumer disputes, supporting the idea of consumer rights in dispute resolution, though it doesn't explicitly mention attorney's fees. The Omission of the fee recovery clause directly weakens consumer protection under the warranty act.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws as determined by the Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: By giving the Buyer the power to determine which laws govern the agreement, the modified text could allow the Buyer to select laws that are unfavorable to the Stockholder, potentially violating the principle of fairness and contradicting laws that protect parties from contracts of adhesion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Giving the Buyer sole discretion to determine the governing law opens the door to selecting laws to unfairly disadvantage the Stockholder, violating this principle of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Buyer to determine the governing law, which could lead to the selection of laws that unfairly disadvantage the Stockholder. This contradicts the UCC § 1-304's obligation of good faith, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By giving the Buyer sole discretion, the modified contract undermines this obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The omission of a neutral and predetermined governing law allows for potential abuse and contradicts the principle of fair dealing, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: It is acknowledged, understood and agreed that the restrictions contained in this Agreement (a) are made for good, valuable and adequate consideration received and to be received by the Stockholder, and (b) are reasonable and necessary, in terms of the time, geographic scope and nature of the restrictions, for the protection of the Buyer and the good will thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: It is acknowledged, understood and agreed that the restrictions contained in this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the Buyer and the good will thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'are made for good, valuable and adequate consideration received and to be received by the Stockholder' can invalidate the non-compete agreement. Non-compete agreements require consideration to be enforceable, and removing acknowledgement of such could allow the Stockholder to challenge the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 71 requires consideration for a contract to be enforceable. By removing the acknowledgement of consideration, the modified text creates doubt as to whether the non-compete agreement is supported by adequate consideration, potentially rendering it unenforceable under contract law principles. The modified URL is to the Restatement itself, as it's a commonly cited and highly respected source on Contract Law. HeinOnline is a very reliable legal database. Note: Direct links to sections of the Restatement online are generally behind paywalls, but the Restatement itself is a widely accepted legal authority.

perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges that the restrictions are 'made for good, valuable and adequate consideration received'. The changed text removes this acknowledgement. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 71 requires consideration for a contract to be enforceable. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that removing this acknowledgement could invalidate the non-compete agreement, as it creates doubt about adequate consideration. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 validates HeinOnline as a reliable source, which is where Restatement can be found. Therefore, removing the acknowledgement of consideration directly contradicts contract law principles regarding enforceability, creating a legal contradiction.
